title: Enable HTTP Strict Transport Security (HSTS)
explanation: Without HSTS, browsers can be tricked into connecting over plain HTTP even when HTTPS is available. This enables SSL stripping attacks.
fix_notes: Add to your server block in nginx.conf or .htaccess. Restart web server after.

title: Add Content Security Policy Header
explanation: Without CSP, attackers can inject malicious scripts into your pages (XSS). CSP tells browsers which sources are trusted.
fix_notes: Start with a restrictive policy and loosen only what breaks. Test in report-only mode first using Content-Security-Policy-Report-Only.

title: Add X-Frame-Options Header (Clickjacking Protection)
explanation: Without this header, attackers can embed your site in an invisible iframe and trick users into clicking hidden buttons.
fix_notes: Use DENY if you never need your site embedded. Use SAMEORIGIN if you embed it yourself.

title: Add X-Content-Type-Options Header
explanation: Without this, browsers may guess the file type and execute a text file as JavaScript.
fix_notes: One line. No downside. Always add this.

title: Add Referrer-Policy Header
explanation: Without this, your URLs (including query strings with sensitive data) are leaked to third-party sites via the Referer header.
fix_notes: Use 'no-referrer' for maximum privacy. 'strict-origin-when-cross-origin' is the recommended balance.

title: Add Permissions-Policy Header
explanation: Without this, any third-party script can request access to camera, microphone, and geolocation.
fix_apache: Header always set Permissions-Policy "camera=(), microphone=(), geolocation=()"
fix_notes: List only the features your site actively uses. Deny everything else.

title: Close Externally Exposed MySQL Port (3306)
explanation: MySQL should NEVER be accessible from the internet. An exposed port 3306 allows brute-force attacks against your database directly.
fix_notes: If your app needs database access, use localhost (127.0.0.1) in your connection string, not the public IP.

title: Close Externally Exposed Redis Port (6379)
explanation: Unauthenticated Redis is a critical vulnerability. Attackers can read all cached data, write arbitrary keys, and in some configs achieve remote code execution.
fix_notes: Edit /etc/redis/redis.conf and set bind to 127.0.0.1. Then restart Redis: sudo systemctl restart redis

title: Close Externally Exposed MongoDB Port (27017)
explanation: An unauthenticated MongoDB instance can expose your entire database to the internet. This has led to thousands of data breaches.
fix_notes: Edit /etc/mongod.conf and set net.bindIp to 127.0.0.1. Restart: sudo systemctl restart mongod

title: Harden SSH Access
explanation: Port 22 exposed to the internet attracts automated brute-force bots within minutes of server creation.
fix_notes: Always test the new SSH connection in a second terminal before closing the first one. Never lock yourself out.

title: Renew SSL Certificate
explanation: An expired certificate breaks HTTPS for all users and shows a security warning in browsers.
fix_bash: echo '0 3 * * * certbot renew --quiet && systemctl reload nginx' | sudo crontab -
fix_notes: Set up auto-renewal immediately. Let's Encrypt certificates expire every 90 days.

title: Disable Directory Listing
explanation: Directory listing shows all files in a folder to anyone who visits. Attackers use this to find backup files, config files, and source code.
fix_notes: Add to your server config or .htaccess file in the web root.

title: Fix CORS Misconfiguration
explanation: A wildcard CORS policy (Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *) allows any website to read your API responses, enabling data theft from logged-in users.
fix_notes: Replace * with your specific frontend domain. Never use * on authenticated APIs.

title: Fix SQL Injection Vulnerability
explanation: SQL injection allows attackers to read, modify, or delete your entire database. This is one of the most critical vulnerabilities possible.
fix_notes: Switch to parameterized queries / prepared statements. Never concatenate user input into SQL strings. Python: cursor.execute('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?', (user_id,)) instead of f'SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = {user_id}'

title: Hide Server Version Information
explanation: Exposing your server version tells attackers exactly which CVEs to use against you.
fix_notes: Also remove X-Powered-By headers from your application framework.

title: Disable Weak TLS Versions
explanation: TLS 1.0 and 1.1 have known vulnerabilities (POODLE, BEAST). Only TLS 1.2 and 1.3 should be enabled.
fix_notes: Test your TLS config at https://www.ssllabs.com/ssltest/ after applying changes.

title: Fix Open Redirect Vulnerability
explanation: Open redirects allow attackers to use your trusted domain to redirect users to malicious sites, making phishing attacks more convincing.
fix_notes: Never redirect to a URL taken directly from user input. Validate that the redirect target is within your own domain. Use a whitelist of allowed redirect destinations.

title: Fix Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
explanation: XSS allows attackers to inject JavaScript into your pages, stealing session cookies, redirecting users, and defacing the site.
fix_notes: Escape all user input before rendering in HTML. Use a templating engine that auto-escapes (Jinja2 autoescaping, React JSX). Implement a strict Content-Security-Policy. Set HttpOnly flag on session cookies.

title: Harden Cookie Security Flags
explanation: Cookies without HttpOnly can be stolen via XSS. Cookies without Secure can be sent over plain HTTP. Cookies without SameSite are vulnerable to CSRF.
fix_notes: Set all session cookies with: HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Strict. In Python/Flask: response.set_cookie('session', value, httponly=True, secure=True, samesite='Strict')

title: Restrict Access to Admin Panel
explanation: A publicly exposed admin panel is a high-value target. Attackers can brute-force it or exploit vulnerabilities in the admin interface directly.
fix_notes: Restrict /admin, /wp-admin, /dashboard to your IP address only. Add multi-factor authentication to all admin accounts.
